Enhancement","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":21,"created_at":"2018-12-09T03:26:25Z","updated_at":"2024-12-01T12:56:41Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"**EDIT: the original issue here was resolved a long time ago, Intel removed their `erf` addition which wasn't in numpy. Issue retitled to reflect the discussion about adding `np.erf`**

<!-- Please describe the issue in detail here, and fill in the fields below -->

When `np.erf` is given a complex number, it returns the number itself instead of the value of the error function. For example,  `scipy.special.erf(1+2j)` returns `(-0.5366435657785664-5.0491437034470374j)` but `np.erf` returns `(1+2j)` itself. 

### Reproducing code example:

<!-- A short code example that reproduces the problem/missing feature. It should be
self-contained, i.e., possible to run as-is via 'python myproblem.py' -->

```python
import numpy as np
print(np.erf(1))     # prints 0.8427007929497149  which is correct 
print(np.erf(1+2j))   # prints (1+2j) which is incorrect
```

<!-- Remove these sections for a feature request -->

### Error message:

None, but the return value is wrong for complex arguments. It is simply the argument itself. If complex inputs are not supported, an error should be thrown instead.

can generate by following
https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/master/doc/source/dev/development_environment.rst#debugging -->

Drop PyPy","user":{"login":"h-vetinari","id":33685575,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMzNjg1NTc1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/33685575?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/h-vetinari","html_url":"https://github.com/h-vetinari","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/h-vetinari/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/h-vetinari/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/h-vetinari/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/h-vetinari/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/h-vetinari/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/h-vetinari/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/h-vetinari/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/h-vetinari/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/h-vetinari/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":17,"created_at":"2024-08-04T10:35:02Z","updated_at":"2024-12-01T20:49:48Z","closed_at":"2024-08-28T13:57:50Z","author_association":"MEMBER","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"body":"It gives me no pleasure to write this, but the signs have been mounting for some time.

Since the [introduction](https://conda-forge.org/blog/2020/03/10/pypy/) of PyPy in conda-forge ~4.5 years ago, we've migrated for 3.6, 3.7, 3.8 & 3.9, introduced windows support halfway through, and made hundreds of packages available pre-compiled for PyPy, or even PyPy-compatible upstream in the first place.

The lion's share of the work there was done by @mattip, one of the PyPy devs, who was truly tireless in getting this effort off the ground. Huge kudos for this work! But unsurprisingly, no single person can keep this up forever, and since the rest of cf/core does not have a lot of PyPy expertise (much less spare time), it all came down predominantly to Matti. When signs of fatigue showed up, I proposed to drop the least common architectures for PyPy
https://github.com/conda-forge/conda-forge.github.io/blob/14deb66144d112a52d38b9ff62f6c6673a105fb7/community/minutes/2023-10-04.md?plain=1#L55-L58

This was rejected at the time, but the situation hasn't changed since then. The linked [issue](https://github.com/conda-forge/pypy3.6-feedstock/issues/104) is a year old, but not much has changed since then. In particular, we have never migrated for PyPy 3.10, which is becoming an issue because many NEP-29 packages are starting to drop Python 3.9 support, and more importantly, we de-scoped PyPy from the numpy 2.0 migration [because](https://github.com/conda-forge/scipy-feedstock/pull/274#issuecomment-2092041382) we concluded
> @mattip: Maybe it is time to drop PyPy from future migrations. Packages that have been built should continue to work, so current users would not be suddenly cut off, and maintainers would not have to deal with the extra headache. I don't see a PyPy3.10 migration happening, and 3.9 is being dropped in the scientific python ecosystem. 

When this briefly came up in a core call a few months ago (not minuted AFAIR), dropping PyPy was already considered an option. I think it's time to make this official. If desired, we could draw this out until we close the numpy 2.0 migration, but in the meantime I think we should announce that we're sunsetting support and stop the PyPy migrator.

No matter the outcome, again a huge thanks to @conda-forge/pypy3-6 @conda-forge/pypy-meta, and @mattip & @isuruf in particular! 👏 🫡 While running a CI workflow for one of my Python projects on GitHub, I noticed that the job that was running Python 3.13 on the current `windows-latest` image was failing. All other Python versions and OS combinations I tests against ({3.10,3.11,3.12} x {ubuntu-latest, macos-latest}) are passing.

Example for a failed workflow run: https://github.com/jenskeiner/exchange_calendars_extensions/actions/runs/12139710770/job/33848270536?pr=119

The issue on the failing job seems to be that importing numpy fails with an exception in `_core/getlimits.py`. The error message "Windows fatal exception: access violation" seems to indicate that the code somehow triggers an illegal access to some ressource.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
# To the best of my knowledge, it should be enough to just import numpy in the 
# problematic environment to trigger the issue. I am not 100% sure though as I 
# can't reproduce the issue locally due to not being able to run the exact same 
# environment.
import numpy
```

While running a CI workflow for one of my Python projects on GitHub, I noticed that the job that was running Python 3.13 on the current `windows-latest` image was failing. All other Python versions and OS combinations I tests against ({3.10,3.11,3.12} x {ubuntu-latest, macos-latest}) are passing.

Example for a failed workflow run: https://github.com/jenskeiner/exchange_calendars_extensions/actions/runs/12139710770/job/33848270536?pr=119

The issue on the failing job seems to be that importing numpy fails with an exception in `_core/getlimits.py`. The error message "Windows fatal exception: access violation" seems to indicate that the code somehow triggers an illegal access to some ressource.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
# To the best of my knowledge, it should be enough to just import numpy in the 
# problematic environment to trigger the issue. I am not 100% sure though as I 
# can't reproduce the issue locally due to not being able to run the exact same 
# environment.
import numpy
```

Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2024-12-04T02:56:21Z","updated_at":"2024-12-04T19:37:24Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

`np.dot` result changes based on shape when the inputs contains`nan`

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np

print(np.dot(np.array([[np.nan]]), np.array(0.)))
print(np.dot(np.array([[np.nan, np.nan]]), np.array(0.)))
```


### Error message:

```shell
[[nan]]
[[0. 0.]]
```

### Context for the issue:

This issue may be related to two previous issues in 2019:

Changes
- Added `coverage` flag in `test`

### Example
```
$ spin test --coverage -- -v -k numpy/tests/test_numpy_config.py 
Removing `/workspaces/numpy/build/coverage`
Invoking `build` prior to running tests:

.
.
.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TOTAL                                                     109351  13626    88%
Coverage HTML written to dir /workspaces/numpy/build/coverage
```

In Spin, after merging of https://github.com/scientific-python/spin/pull/146:

![image](https://github.com/scientific-python/spin/assets/20969920/1ed0887f-3875-4846-a9ae-47ad5aab39c4)

1. `spin test --coverage --gcov`
2. `spin test --generate-lcov-html`
```
~/os/numpy (bld_24080_coverage) » spin test --generate-lcov-html                                                                                                            ganesh@ganesh-MS-7B86
Deleting old HTML coverage report...
Generating HTML coverage report...
$ ninja coverage-html -C build
Coverage report generated successfully and written to /home/ganesh/os/numpy/build/meson-logs/coveragereport/index.html
```

---

Other error handling
1. `spin build`
2. `spin test --generate-lcov-html`
```
~/os/numpy (bld_24080_coverage) » spin test --generate-lcov-html                                                                                                            ganesh@ganesh-MS-7B86
Error: GCOV files missing... Cannot generate coverage reports. Coverage reports can be generated by `spin test --coverage --gcov`

```

---


### Notes

Question","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":"Question about NumPy usage or development"}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":20,"created_at":"2024-06-16T19:50:03Z","updated_at":"2024-12-05T11:53:10Z","closed_at":"2024-10-26T20:29:40Z","author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

We have a rather comprehensive CI suite of tests running for Airflow, and after Numpy 2.0.0 release, Python 3.12 tests of Airflow started to fail with this error:

```
ValueError: numpy.dtype size changed, may indicate binary incompatibility. Expected 96 from C header, got 88 from PyObject
```

It seems to be triggered by `pypi` resolving Pandas + numpy requirements to upgrade Numpy to 2.0.0 and downgrade pandas to 2.1.1 (from 2.1.4). It's likely because pandas 2.1.1 does not have limitation for numpy to be `<2` (pandas 2.1.4 actually has the limit added).

This is the diff of installation of "eager-upgrade" we run in Airflow's CI:

```diff
355c355
< numpy==1.26.4
---
> numpy==2.0.0
381c381
< pandas==2.1.4
---
> pandas==2.1.1
```

And **just** importing pandas -> numpy causes this error. Example failure (https://github.com/apache/airflow/actions/runs/9537986684/job/26286749479) 

```python

```

It happens on ly for Python 3.12 - for Python 3.8-3.11, eager upgrade does not cause new numpy 2.0.0 to be installed. It's likely **some** incompatibility between pandas/numpy. We are going to address it temporarily for Airlfow by limiting numpy to < 2.0.0 until this is solved, so nothing urgent but maybe you can figure out some ways this can be prevented - seems that when `pip` is presented with command to install `numpy` and `pandas` on Python 3.12 will install `numpy 2.1.1` and `pandas 2.0.0` and they won't work together.



### Reproduce the code example:

```python
* Have a Python 3.12 environment
* Install pandas 2.1.1 and numpy 2.0.0:


pip install pandas==2.1.1 numpy==2.0.0
pip check


* Everything is fine
* Import pandas

ValueError: numpy.dtype size changed, may indicate binary incompatibility. Expected 96 from C header, got 88 from PyObject
```

Not sure if anything can be done there, we are going to change our limits to exclude that combination, but maybe you can also take a look and see if some exclusions might be needed.

So it is bound to be confusing. With that, we are always open to improvements. We could expand the [Understanding the code & getting started](https://numpy.org/devdocs/dev/development_environment.html#understanding-the-code-getting-started) section of the documents with a high-level overview of how the different Python and C-API pieces fit together. Is that something you would be willing to help us with? Let's dive into your questions/complaints:

> what if a person wants to cooperate with you

We have various ways of getting in touch with the team. There are [meetings](https://scientific-python.org/calendars/) of the documentation, optimization, community, and triage groups, as well as a new contributor's hour. There is this issue tracker, and the github discussions. There is a [mailing list](https://mail.python.org/mailman3/lists/numpy-discussion.python.org/).

> there is no documentation related to a developer who wants to see which part of your codes are the core, which are modules

The best way to do this, like in any python library, is to dive in and check. For instance, this will show you the modules (again, this is generic to any library):
```
[x for x in dir(np) if isinstance(getattr(np, x), type(np.random))]
```

I am not sure what you mean by "core", but two of the core concepts in NumPy are `np.ndarray` and `np.ufunc`. You can read about them in the [documentation](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/index.html).

> how do you manage match operation, where to begin to understand 

Can you be more specific? I am not sure NumPy is any different from other libraries that wrap C code. One complication is that we decorate the [Array API](https://data-apis.org/array-api/latest/) functions with a wrapper. So to find the C implementation for `dot`, you would look at `np.dot.__wrapped__`, which is `built-in function dot`. Like any library that uses the Python C-API, this means you need to find `"dot"`, which can be done with `git grep '"dot"'` (note the use of quotes), which will point to both `numpy/_core/src/multiarray/methods.c` and `numpy/_core/src/multiarray/multiarraymodule.c`. The former is the `np.ndarray` method, the latter is the `np` function.
https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/1b2b17afd6d63bd4520c7f4d13e8498479cfbc45/numpy/_core/src/multiarray/methods.c#L2939-L2941 https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/1b2b17afd6d63bd4520c7f4d13e8498479cfbc45/numpy/_core/src/multiarray/multiarraymodule.c#L4456-L4458

From there you need to look at the actual implementation of those functions in C. Crossing the python -> C debugging bridge is tricky, but again not specific to NumPy.

> this is absolutely the opposite of open-source philosophy...

I am not sure where you are going with the rest of this, so I will let others answer it.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2525291568/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-12-08T01:37:56Z","updated_at":"2024-12-08T06:18:17Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

I'm trying to install numpy for another repo [freqtrade](https://github.com/freqtrade/freqtrade). It requires numpy v1.26.4, and I'm in the process of verifying the issue on a more recent release v2.1.3.

I'm trying to set up a system to do trading stuff on an old computer, namely a system with a Pentium Pro CPU and 128 MB of RAM. The features of the CPU are extremely limited since the Pentium Pro has NO SIMD extensions. I found a page on your support forum (https://numpy.org/doc/2.1/reference/simd/build-options.html) about compiling numpy for a host system and tried to do that namely:

```
python -m build --wheel -Csetup-args=-Dcpu-baseline="native" -Csetup-args=-Dcpu-dispatch="none"
```

To which I can get the build system to skip trying to detect SIMD extensions, since for some reason it still finds them even though they aren't supported by my processor? I think that may be a different bug wrt the `detect` system detecting unsupported features.

Here is the output of `cat /proc/cpuinfo`
```

bogomips	: 398.85

address sizes	: 36 bits physical, 32 bits virtual
power management:

```

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
wget https://files.pythonhosted.org/packages/source/n/numpy/numpy-1.26.4.tar.gz
tar -xvf ./numpy-1.26.4.tar.gz
cd numpy-1.26.4
python -m build --wheel -Csetup-args=-Dcpu-baseline="native" -Csetup-args=-Dcpu-dispatch="none"
```


### Error message:

```shell
[272/311] Generating numpy/random/_bounded_integer_pxd with a custom command

```


### Python and NumPy Versions:

python: 3.11.2
numpy: 1.26.4

### Runtime Environment:

I created a virtual environment with the following packages installed:
```
meson 
ninja 
cython==0.29.34  # this is important as newer versions of cython contain illegal instructions as well
build
```

### Context for the issue:

It doesn't really block my work, as I have other systems, but I feel that it should be important that numpy can be compiled without SIMD extensions.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27929/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27929/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2525441059","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27929#issuecomment-2525441059","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27929","id":2525441059,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP86Whywj","user":{"login":"mattip","id":823911,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgyMzkxMQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/823911?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip","html_url":"https://github.com/mattip","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-08T06:18:16Z","updated_at":"2024-12-08T06:18:16Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"I think the problem is SSE2. [That processor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_Intel_Pentium_Pro_processors), whose last iteration came out over 25 years ago, and only supports 32-bit operating systems, lacks any SSE support. I am pretty sure something in compilation of NumPy requires SSE2 but could not find documentation that confirms that. Perhaps we should make that explicit somewhere. In any case, you cannot use any OpenBLAS since 0.26. PR #27851 suggests moving to a minimum of SSE4.1, which would also preclude all "Pentium 4" and other Prescott processors before ["Sandy Bridge"](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_Intel_Pentium_processors) released in 2011. 

If your stack requires NumPy 1.26.4, I doubt the other components will work with a 32-bit CPU without any SSE support. For fun you could try going back in versions with the NumPy wheels on PyPI to find a version that works. You may need to go back as far as 1.7, since I see [1.8 already uses SSE2 loops](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/release/1.8.0-notes.html#performance-improvements-via-sse2-vectorization) but they may be optional if you build your own version from source. Note that version 1.7.2 provides wheels for Python<3.4.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2525441059/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Define the macro when not already defined.

xref https://gitlab.alpinelinux.org/alpine/aports/-/blob/v3.21.0/community/py3-numpy/s390x-hwcap.patch?ref_type=tags

fix #27932

The second commit can be removed or kept pending review. It uses the kernel HWCAP names for s390 which are defined in musl libc and recent glibc versions (>= 2.33):
```
find /usr -name '*.h' -exec grep -H HWCAP_S390_VX {} \;

glibc 2.17
/usr/include/bits/hwcap.h:#define HWCAP_S390_VX           2048
/usr/include/bits/hwcap.h:#define HWCAP_S390_VXD          4096
/usr/include/bits/hwcap.h:#define HWCAP_S390_VXE          8192

glibc 2.34
/usr/include/bits/hwcap.h:#define HWCAP_S390_VX           2048
/usr/include/bits/hwcap.h:#define HWCAP_S390_VXRS         HWCAP_S390_VX
/usr/include/bits/hwcap.h:#define HWCAP_S390_VXD          4096
/usr/include/bits/hwcap.h:#define HWCAP_S390_VXRS_BCD     HWCAP_S390_VXD
/usr/include/bits/hwcap.h:#define HWCAP_S390_VXE          8192
/usr/include/bits/hwcap.h:#define HWCAP_S390_VXRS_EXT     HWCAP_S390_VXE
/usr/include/bits/hwcap.h:#define HWCAP_S390_VXRS_EXT2    32768
/usr/include/bits/hwcap.h:#define HWCAP_S390_VXRS_PDE     65536
/usr/include/bits/hwcap.h:#define HWCAP_S390_VXRS_PDE2    524288

musl 1.2
/usr/include/bits/hwcap.h:#define HWCAP_S390_VXRS		2048
/usr/include/bits/hwcap.h:#define HWCAP_S390_VXRS_BCD	4096
/usr/include/bits/hwcap.h:#define HWCAP_S390_VXRS_EXT	8192
```","created_at":"2024-12-08T10:12:23Z","updated_at":"2024-12-08T11:46:58Z","closed_at":"2024-12-08T11:46:58Z","merged_at":"2024-12-08T11:46:58Z","merge_commit_sha":"75f64cac922681ce5132eee4b8977f9611fba8ab","assignee":null,"assignees":[],"requested_reviewers":[],"requested_teams":[],"labels":[{"id":6359214,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw2MzU5MjE0","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/00%20-%20Bug","name":"00 Failure to build on Alpine Linux s390x where `HWCAP_S390_VX` is not defined.
There's a patch downstream which won't work on glibc<2.33: https://gitlab.alpinelinux.org/alpine/aports/-/blob/v3.21.0/community/py3-numpy/s390x-hwcap.patch?ref_type=tags

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
in a musllinux_1_2_s390x container:
apk add openblas-dev
pip wheel -w $(pwd) --no-binary=numpy numpy==2.1.1
```

Define the macro when not already defined.

xref https://gitlab.alpinelinux.org/alpine/aports/-/blob/v3.21.0/community/py3-numpy/s390x-hwcap.patch?ref_type=tags

fix #27932

The second commit can be removed or kept pending review. It uses the kernel HWCAP names for s390 which are defined in musl libc and recent glibc versions (>= 2.33):
```
find /usr -name '*.h' -exec grep -H HWCAP_S390_VX {} \;

glibc 2.17
/usr/include/bits/hwcap.h:#define HWCAP_S390_VX           2048
/usr/include/bits/hwcap.h:#define HWCAP_S390_VXD          4096
/usr/include/bits/hwcap.h:#define HWCAP_S390_VXE          8192

glibc 2.34
/usr/include/bits/hwcap.h:#define HWCAP_S390_VX           2048
/usr/include/bits/hwcap.h:#define HWCAP_S390_VXRS         HWCAP_S390_VX
/usr/include/bits/hwcap.h:#define HWCAP_S390_VXD          4096
/usr/include/bits/hwcap.h:#define HWCAP_S390_VXRS_BCD     HWCAP_S390_VXD
/usr/include/bits/hwcap.h:#define HWCAP_S390_VXE          8192
/usr/include/bits/hwcap.h:#define HWCAP_S390_VXRS_EXT     HWCAP_S390_VXE
/usr/include/bits/hwcap.h:#define HWCAP_S390_VXRS_EXT2    32768
/usr/include/bits/hwcap.h:#define HWCAP_S390_VXRS_PDE     65536
/usr/include/bits/hwcap.h:#define HWCAP_S390_VXRS_PDE2    524288

musl 1.2
/usr/include/bits/hwcap.h:#define HWCAP_S390_VXRS		2048
/usr/include/bits/hwcap.h:#define HWCAP_S390_VXRS_BCD	4096
/usr/include/bits/hwcap.h:#define HWCAP_S390_VXRS_EXT	8192
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27933/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27933/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2525678500","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27933#issuecomment-2525678500","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27933","id":2525678500,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP86Wisuk","user":{"login":"mattip","id":823911,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgyMzkxMQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/823911?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip","html_url":"https://github.com/mattip","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-08T11:47:08Z","updated_at":"2024-12-08T11:47:08Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"Thanks @mayeut","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2525678500/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

I'm writing a loader for the npy file format is a non-Python language.  Part of the header of this file is a JSON encoded dictionary.  I am unable to parse it in my other language because the JSON numpy is generating does not conform to the JSON standard.  

https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.lib.format.html

Specifically, the JSON standard requires:
- double quotes for string literals
- square brackets for arrays
- lower case for true and false boolean values

The .npy file header uses
- single quotes for string literals
- round brackets for arrays
- capitalized True and False for boolean values

Dictionary extracted from the .npy file I generated:
```
{'descr': '<f8', 'fortran_order': False, 'shape': (10, 50, 60, 70), }
```

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np
import opensimplex

feature_size = 10
size_x = 70
size_y = 60
size_z = 50
size_w = 10

ix = np.arange(0, feature_size, feature_size / size_x)
iy = np.arange(0, feature_size, feature_size / size_y)
iz = np.arange(0, feature_size, feature_size / size_z)
iw = np.arange(0, feature_size, feature_size / size_w)

arr = opensimplex.noise4array(ix, iy, iz, iw)
arr = (arr + 1) * .5

np.save('noise_data_4d.npy', arr)
```

recently discovered [zizmor](https://woodruffw.github.io/zizmor/), a tool to audit github workflows. It exposed some things we should change to make our workflows more secure. Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2024-12-09T10:41:33Z","updated_at":"2024-12-09T12:59:37Z","closed_at":"2024-12-09T10:56:10Z","author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

If I multiply a Python integer by a numpy.int32 value, then on my Linux machine the result is a numpy.int64 and on Windows it's a numpy.int32. This results in platform specific integer overflow problems.

I have tried
   Linux(Ubuntu): Python 3.12.3/numpy 1.26.4
   Windows:  Python 3.12.3/numpy 1.26.4 and Python 3.13.1/numpy 2.2.0


### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import sys
import platform
import numpy as np

print(platform.uname())
print(sys.version)
print('numpy:', np.__version__)
print()

a=100
b=np.int32(10**8)
print(type(a*b), type(b*a))
```


### Error message:

```shell
On Linux no error message occurs
Windows gives "RuntimeWarning: overflow encountered in scalar multiply"

The full output from the code example for the three platforms tested is...

version | OS| a | b (python int) | a * b | Notes
---|---|---|---|---|---
1.26.4 | Linux | `np.int32(100)` | 100 | `np.int64(10_000)` | python int -> int64, result is promoted
1.26.4 | Windows | `np.int32(100)` | 100 | `np.int32(10_000)` | python int -> int32, no promotion
2.0+ | Linux | `np.int32(100)` | 100 | `np.int32(10_000)` | no more promotion
2.0+ | Windows | `np.int32(100)` | 100 | `np.int32(10_000)` | like before, but for different reasons

You will get an overflow warning whenever the return type is int32. {"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27966","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27966/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27966/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27966/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27966","id":2731121766,"node_id":"I_kwDOAA3dP86iyZxm","number":27966,"title":"ENH: add a np.explain context manager to help teach new developers and debug ufunc loops","user":{"login":"mattip","id":823911,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgyMzkxMQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/823911?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip","html_url":"https://github.com/mattip","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-12-10T20:27:57Z","updated_at":"2024-12-10T20:27:57Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Proposed new feature or change:

I have had this idea for some time now about a decorator that would trace calls into ufuncs and linalg, for instance
```
>>> with numpy.explain() as ex:
...    a + b
>>> print(ex)
called np.add -> LONG_addavx512 * 1024 -> LONG_add (not contiguous) *1024
```

The idea would be to provide some traceability into what NumPy is doing in a way that would help debug performance problems. The trick would be to make the mechanism fast and non-intrusive. I think something like a double linked list of
```
typedef _explainer {
    _explainer * prev;
    _explainer * next;
    const char * msg;
    int count;
} explainer;
```
and pass around the tail of the chain. The first call (in the decorator) would hold the head. Each annotated function would check `if not NULL` and either add a new node with a `const char` hard-coded `msg` or, if the `msg` matches, would increment the count. The context manager would pass in a first node, and would print and clean up memory. If no context manager is used, the NULL checks would be the only overhead.

Benefit:
- Developers could easily test that the code paths they are trying to hit are actually used. This is complicated today, since there is no direct visibility into what is being called.
- This could be used as a teaching tool both for "what is going on" new users and for "diving into NumPy" would-be contributors
- End users who hit a performance snag would be able to see why code is slow. Having the call count would show how non-contiguous data or buffering is harming performance. Having the inner loop name clearly available would show what dtype and loop optimization is actually used in practice.

Risks: 
- this may add too much overhead
- it would require finding a place to "tuck away" the pointer. For new-style ufuncs it would be easy since they have a `context` argument. There may be a way to hide the pointer in the unused `auxdata` arg to old-style ufuncs.
- cleanup under error conditions may be tricky and lead to memory leaks.
- the community may reject it

Next steps:
If this seems like something worth working on, it should hit the mailing list and also get a first working prototype. Then a NEP can be written to hammer out the finer details.

Alternatives:
- use python profilers that "know" about c-extensions like [py-spy](https://github.com/benfred/py-spy/blob/master/README.md) which already exists and can provide nice flame graphs.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27966/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27966/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

Describe the issue:

The following code seems correct, and passes type checking under 2.1.3 but not 2.2.0:

```python
import numpy as np

rng = np.random.default_rng(27446968)
n_states = 5

P = rng.random(size=(n_states, n_states))
p = rng.random(n_states)

p = P.T @ p
```

I suppose mypy is flagging that the float width can increase, but since float64 is used by random, that won't happen in this scenario.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
N/A
```

```shell

Found 1 error in 1 file (checked 1 source file)
```

Enhancement","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2024-12-10T20:27:57Z","updated_at":"2024-12-12T17:57:59Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Proposed new feature or change:

I have had this idea for some time now about a decorator that would trace calls into ufuncs and linalg, for instance
```
>>> with numpy.explain() as ex:
...    a + b
>>> print(ex)
called np.add -> LONG_addavx512 * 1024 -> LONG_add (not contiguous) *1024
```

The idea would be to provide some traceability into what NumPy is doing in a way that would help debug performance problems. The trick would be to make the mechanism fast and non-intrusive. I think something like a double linked list of
```
typedef _explainer {
    _explainer * prev;
    _explainer * next;
    const char * msg;
    int count;
} explainer;
```
and pass around the tail of the chain. The first call (in the decorator) would hold the head. Each annotated function would check `if not NULL` and either add a new node with a `const char` hard-coded `msg` or, if the `msg` matches, would increment the count. The context manager would pass in a first node, and would print and clean up memory. If no context manager is used, the NULL checks would be the only overhead.

Benefit:
- Developers could easily test that the code paths they are trying to hit are actually used. This is complicated today, since there is no direct visibility into what is being called.
- This could be used as a teaching tool both for "what is going on" new users and for "diving into NumPy" would-be contributors
- End users who hit a performance snag would be able to see why code is slow. Having the call count would show how non-contiguous data or buffering is harming performance. Having the inner loop name clearly available would show what dtype and loop optimization is actually used in practice.

Risks: 
- this may add too much overhead
- it would require finding a place to "tuck away" the pointer. For new-style ufuncs it would be easy since they have a `context` argument. There may be a way to hide the pointer in the unused `auxdata` arg to old-style ufuncs.
- cleanup under error conditions may be tricky and lead to memory leaks.
- the community may reject it

Next steps:
If this seems like something worth working on, it should hit the mailing list and also get a first working prototype. Then a NEP can be written to hammer out the finer details.

Alternatives:
- use python profilers that "know" about c-extensions like [py-spy](https://github.com/benfred/py-spy/blob/master/README.md) which already exists and can provide nice flame graphs.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27966/reactions","total_count":3,"+1":3,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27966/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2539667798","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27966#issuecomment-2539667798","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27966","id":2539667798,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP86XYEFW","user":{"login":"mattip","id":823911,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgyMzkxMQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/823911?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip","html_url":"https://github.com/mattip","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-12T17:57:58Z","updated_at":"2024-12-12T17:57:58Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"`np.lib.introspect.opt_func_info`, if I understand correctly how to use it, returns a dictionary of possible optimization levels for a particular ufunc:

Over at #28001 we discovered that `np.dtype.kind` is not being tested via doctests. I think the problem is in doctests itself, where [it only checks certain items in `obj.__dict__`](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/7900a85019457c14e8c6abac532846bc9f26760d/Lib/doctest.py#L1064):
- staticmethod, classmethod, property
- inspect.isroutine, inspect.isclass

In the case at hand, `np.dtype.kind` is a member, so it is not collected for testing.

### Idea or request for content:

We should find a work-around, as doctest is a part of the python stdlib, so we cannot simply upgrade the version. cc @ev-br ","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/28002/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/28002/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

: `np.dtype.kind` docstring is not checked.

```
In [4]: type(np.dtype.kind)
Out[4]: member_descriptor
```

and this `member_descriptor` is not recognized as an `inspect.routine` by `doctest.DoctestFinder._find`. There are in fact several other `something_descriptor`s in np.dtype methods, and they are also skipped:

```

Out[3]:

https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.dtype.type.html
... has:
numpy.dtype.type
attribute
dtype.type = None

Is there something missing here?  For example, if I go:
```
>>> import numpy as np
>>> A = np.array([1.0, 2, 3])
>>> A.dtype.type
<class 'numpy.float64'>
>>>
```
...  `<class 'numpy.float64'>` is not `None`?

Describe the issue:

The following code seems correct, and passes type checking under 2.1.3 but not 2.2.0:

```python
import numpy as np

rng = np.random.default_rng(27446968)
n_states = 5

P = rng.random(size=(n_states, n_states))
p = rng.random(n_states)

p = P.T @ p
```

I suppose mypy is flagging that the float width can increase, but since float64 is used by random, that won't happen in this scenario.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
N/A
```


### Error message:

```shell
foo.py:10: error: Incompatible types in assignment (expression has type "ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[floating[Any]]]", variable has type "ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[float64]]")  [assignment]
Found 1 error in 1 file (checked 1 source file)
```


### Python and NumPy Versions:

Python 3.13, mypy 1.13, numpy 2.2.0

### Runtime Environment:

setup","user":{"login":"seberg","id":61977,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYxOTc3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/61977?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg","html_url":"https://github.com/seberg","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"This changes the buffered iteration setup to do most of the work initially rather than when actually copying buffers.

It has a few changes:
- As of now, a very slightly higher overhead initially.
- Shrinks the buffersize to fit the dimensions.  This means that buffers can be re-used far more often for broadcast operations.
  (To be fair, this is most important with reductions where the buffersize always had to be shrunk down.)
- The buffer re-use logic is now passably understandable, I hope.  Which means we may also be able to optimize it further (e.g. allow re-use for dtypes with references, which currently fails...).
- Tries to guess the whether using buffers is actually worthwhile.  And example is:
  ```
  a = np.ones((4, 500))[::2]
  %timeit a + a  # 840ns vs. 1.15
  ```
  so not sure it is important, could maybe safe on it and just grow as much as possible.
  (A possible new optimization: Use "reduce" loop even if not a reduce.)


It still needs:
- [x] Figure out why CI breaks down when local run did not.
- [x] (largely ok) A bit of cleanup (e.g. unnecessary moving around).  The main function is long and a bit messy, I admit.  But a second pair of eyes is probably better at suggesting improvements if they are worthwhile.
- [ ] Someone will probably want some good speedup examples.  They exist, but I need to nail down the nicest ones.
- [x] Decide what to do about changes the buffered iteration setup to do most of the work initially rather than when actually copying buffers.

It has a few changes:
- As of now, a very slightly higher overhead initially.
- Shrinks the buffersize to fit the dimensions.  This means that buffers can be re-used far more often for broadcast operations.
  (To be fair, this is most important with reductions where the buffersize always had to be shrunk down.)
- The buffer re-use logic is now passably understandable, I hope.  Which means we may also be able to optimize it further (e.g. allow re-use for dtypes with references, which currently fails...).
- Tries to guess the whether using buffers is actually worthwhile.  And example is:
  ```
  a = np.ones((4, 500))[::2]
  %timeit a + a  # 840ns vs. 1.15
  ```
  so not sure it is important, could maybe safe on it and just grow as much as possible.
  (A possible new optimization: Use "reduce" loop even if not a reduce.)


It still needs:
- [x] Figure out why CI breaks down when local run did not.
- [x] (largely ok) A bit of cleanup (e.g. unnecessary moving around).  The main function is long and a bit messy, I admit.  But a second pair of eyes is probably better at suggesting improvements if they are worthwhile.
- [ ] Someone will probably want some good speedup examples.  They exist, but I need to nail down the nicest ones.
- [x] Decide what to do about changes the buffered iteration setup to do most of the work initially rather than when actually copying buffers.

It has a few changes:
- As of now, a very slightly higher overhead initially.
- Shrinks the buffersize to fit the dimensions.  This means that buffers can be re-used far more often for broadcast operations.
  (To be fair, this is most important with reductions where the buffersize always had to be shrunk down.)
- The buffer re-use logic is now passably understandable, I hope.  Which means we may also be able to optimize it further (e.g. allow re-use for dtypes with references, which currently fails...).
- Tries to guess the whether using buffers is actually worthwhile.  And example is:
  ```
  a = np.ones((4, 500))[::2]
  %timeit a + a  # 840ns vs. 1.15
  ```
  so not sure it is important, could maybe safe on it and just grow as much as possible.
  (A possible new optimization: Use "reduce" loop even if not a reduce.)


It still needs:

setup","user":{"login":"seberg","id":61977,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYxOTc3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/61977?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg","html_url":"https://github.com/seberg","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"This changes the buffered iteration setup to do most of the work initially rather than when actually copying buffers.

It has a few changes:
- As of now, a very slightly higher overhead initially.
- Shrinks the buffersize to fit the dimensions.  This means that buffers can be re-used far more often for broadcast operations.
  (To be fair, this is most important with reductions where the buffersize always had to be shrunk down.)
- The buffer re-use logic is now passably understandable, I hope.  Which means we may also be able to optimize it further (e.g. allow re-use for dtypes with references, which currently fails...).
- Tries to guess the whether using buffers is actually worthwhile.  And example is:
  ```
  a = np.ones((4, 500))[::2]
  %timeit a + a  # 840ns vs. 1.15
  ```
  so not sure it is important, could maybe safe on it and just grow as much as possible.
  (A possible new optimization: Use "reduce" loop even if not a reduce.)


It still needs:
- [x] Figure out why CI breaks down when local run did not.
- [x] (largely ok) A bit of cleanup (e.g. unnecessary moving around).  The main function is long and a bit messy, I admit.  But a second pair of eyes is probably better at suggesting improvements if they are worthwhile.
- [ ] Someone will probably want some good speedup examples.  They exist, but I need to nail down the nicest ones.
- [x] Decide what to do about changes the buffered iteration setup to do most of the work initially rather than when actually copying buffers.

It has a few changes:
- As of now, a very slightly higher overhead initially.
- Shrinks the buffersize to fit the dimensions.  This means that buffers can be re-used far more often for broadcast operations.
  (To be fair, this is most important with reductions where the buffersize always had to be shrunk down.)
- The buffer re-use logic is now passably understandable, I hope.  Which means we may also be able to optimize it further (e.g. allow re-use for dtypes with references, which currently fails...).
- Tries to guess the whether using buffers is actually worthwhile.  And example is:
  ```
  a = np.ones((4, 500))[::2]
  %timeit a + a  # 840ns vs. 1.15
  ```
  so not sure it is important, could maybe safe on it and just grow as much as possible.
  (A possible new optimization: Use "reduce" loop even if not a reduce.)


It still needs:
- [x] Figure out why CI breaks down when local run did not.
- [x] (largely ok) A bit of cleanup (e.g. unnecessary moving around).  The main function is long and a bit messy, I admit.  But a second pair of eyes is probably better at suggesting improvements if they are worthwhile.
- [ ] Someone will probably want some good speedup examples.  They exist, but I need to nail down the nicest ones.
- [x] Decide what to do about Maintenance","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":5,"created_at":"2024-12-01T22:12:59Z","updated_at":"2024-12-17T09:38:57Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/27883","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27883","diff_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27883.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27883.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This changes the buffered iteration setup to do most of the work initially rather than when actually copying buffers.

It has a few changes:
- As of now, a very slightly higher overhead initially.
- Shrinks the buffersize to fit the dimensions.  This means that buffers can be re-used far more often for broadcast operations.
  (To be fair, this is most important with reductions where the buffersize always had to be shrunk down.)
- The buffer re-use logic is now passably understandable, I hope.  Which means we may also be able to optimize it further (e.g. allow re-use for dtypes with references, which currently fails...).
- Tries to guess the whether using buffers is actually worthwhile.  And example is:
  ```
  a = np.ones((4, 500))[::2]
  %timeit a + a  # 840ns vs. 1.15
  ```
  so not sure it is important, could maybe safe on it and just grow as much as possible.
  (A possible new optimization: Use "reduce" loop even if not a reduce.)


It still needs:
- [x] Figure out why CI breaks down when local run did not.
- [x] (largely ok) A bit of cleanup (e.g. unnecessary moving around).  The main function is long and a bit messy, I admit.  But a second pair of eyes is probably better at suggesting improvements if they are worthwhile.
- [ ] Someone will probably want some good speedup examples.  They exist, but I need to nail down the nicest ones.
- [x] Decide what to do about `NPY_ITER_CONTIG`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27883/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27883/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2547951900","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27883#issuecomment-2547951900","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27883","id":2547951900,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP86X3qkc","user":{"login":"mattip","id":823911,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgyMzkxMQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/823911?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip","html_url":"https://github.com/mattip","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-17T09:38:56Z","updated_at":"2024-12-17T09:38:56Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"There are some follow ups, but nothing really jumps out as a possible continuation. {"repository_id":908607,"push_id":21746015711,"size":24,"distinct_size":24,"ref":"refs/heads/main","head":"f36fa72c2c0d8efb2d17662cb9ddb846b6a9cf24","before":"837121612fea0128e1b4703889c01f77d2cbf2e8","commits":[{"sha":"8d69f05ddf190fd8ea6e2d896772f5aac607f6b8","author":{"email":"d426ac32e079de9b556f042265d1feb9211a449d@nvidia.com","name":"Sebastian Berg"},"message":"MAINT: Refactor buffered loop to do an initial setup

This reorganizes the loop to do a larger initial setup rather than
figuring things out on every iteration.
That shrinks the buffersize often to fit better with the actual size.
We try to do that smartly, although, the logic is different.

It also means that "reduce" is not necessarily used (although it
is likely that before it was also effectively unused often).

This is not generally faster, but in some cases it will not use
buffering unnecessarily and it should be much better at buffer
re-use (at least with smaller fixes).
In those cases things will be much faster.

(As of writing, the setup time seems slightly slower, that is probably
not unexpected, since finding the best size is extra work and it would
only amortize if there are many iterations.)","distinct":true,"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/commits/8d69f05ddf190fd8ea6e2d896772f5aac607f6b8"},{"sha":"7d19f2dcbd8bb6fe51a448f2f96ba4fbfd74ad0a","author":{"email":"d426ac32e079de9b556f042265d1feb9211a449d@nvidia.com","name":"Sebastian Berg"},"message":"TST: Fixup tests to pass (for now not correct)","distinct":true,"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/commits/7d19f2dcbd8bb6fe51a448f2f96ba4fbfd74ad0a"},{"sha":"809cf0cdde7555171739e70fe4de0ec750a1df1f","author":{"email":"d426ac32e079de9b556f042265d1feb9211a449d@nvidia.com","name":"Sebastian Berg"},"message":"BUG: Avoid a coresize of 0","distinct":true,"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/commits/809cf0cdde7555171739e70fe4de0ec750a1df1f"},{"sha":"9302b303957d63b06a1bb5a85bd559339b361ea3","author":{"email":"d426ac32e079de9b556f042265d1feb9211a449d@nvidia.com","name":"Sebastian Berg"},"message":"TST: The nullpointer doesn't print uniformly accross platforms","distinct":true,"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/commits/9302b303957d63b06a1bb5a85bd559339b361ea3"},{"sha":"54d967f21cc61be228eb4f3b72e7d67d8ba85e3f","author":{"email":"d426ac32e079de9b556f042265d1feb9211a449d@nvidia.com","name":"Sebastian Berg"},"message":"BUG: Add missing pointer dereference

It has a few changes:
- As of now, a very slightly higher overhead initially.
- Shrinks the buffersize to fit the dimensions.  This means that buffers can be re-used far more often for broadcast operations.
  (To be fair, this is most important with reductions where the buffersize always had to be shrunk down.)
- The buffer re-use logic is now passably understandable, I hope.  Which means we may also be able to optimize it further (e.g. allow re-use for dtypes with references, which currently fails...).
- Tries to guess the whether using buffers is actually worthwhile.  And example is:
  ```
  a = np.ones((4, 500))[::2]
  %timeit a + a  # 840ns vs. 1.15
  ```
  so not sure it is important, could maybe safe on it and just grow as much as possible.
  (A possible new optimization: Use "reduce" loop even if not a reduce.)


It still needs:
- [x] Figure out why CI breaks down when local run did not.
- [x] (largely ok) A bit of cleanup (e.g. unnecessary moving around).  The main function is long and a bit messy, I admit.  But a second pair of eyes is probably better at suggesting improvements if they are worthwhile.
- [ ] Someone will probably want some good speedup examples.  They exist, but I need to nail down the nicest ones.
- [x] Decide what to do about is just because I found the way things were iterated confusing and I think this is a lot less confusing and I can't imagine it's slower.

---

The pointers were always stored for each axis (pointing to the start of the whatever the index into the axis was). I found that confusing: One set of pointers is enough.

But some gufuncs relied on external loop giving a second set of pointers (buffers always do so ufuncs are fine).
So, merged the buffer pointers and the external loop into a new field.

I hope (but not sure) that compilers won't complain about loops that are never taken (didn't notice it locally, but...).

For speed testing, I tried to time with something like:
```
a = np.broadcast_to(np.zeros((2, 4))[:, ::2], (100000, 2, 2))

%timeit np.count_nonzero(a)
%timeit np.count_nonzero(a[..., 0])

%timeit np.nonzero(a)
%timeit np.nonzero(a[..., 0])
```
Especially the first (count nonzero).  That code uses the external loop path without buffering (i.e. what changed most). Locally, saw a small slowdown with nonzero, but on a different box and if anything I think a slight speedup for count-nonzero.

So, I doubt this makes a difference, of course an iteration only C test might see a tiny bit more of a difference.


EDIT: Actually, on my old laptop that has (without turbo-boosting) more stable benchmarks, it's better in all cases.  (of course architectures could behave slightly iternext","user":{"login":"seberg","id":61977,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYxOTc3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/61977?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg","html_url":"https://github.com/seberg","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"This is just because I found the way things were iterated confusing and I think this is a lot less confusing and I can't imagine it's slower.

---

The pointers were always stored for each axis (pointing to the start of the whatever the index into the axis was). I found that confusing: One set of pointers is enough.

But some gufuncs relied on external loop giving a second set of pointers (buffers always do so ufuncs are fine).
So, merged the buffer pointers and the external loop into a new field.

I hope (but not sure) that compilers won't complain about loops that are never taken (didn't notice it locally, but...).

For speed testing, I tried to time with something like:
```
a = np.broadcast_to(np.zeros((2, 4))[:, ::2], (100000, 2, 2))

%timeit np.count_nonzero(a)
%timeit np.count_nonzero(a[..., 0])

%timeit np.nonzero(a)
%timeit np.nonzero(a[..., 0])
```
Especially the first (count nonzero).  That code uses the external loop path without buffering (i.e. what changed most). Locally, saw a small slowdown with nonzero, but on a different box and if anything I think a slight speedup for count-nonzero.

So, I doubt this makes a difference, of course an iteration only C test might see a tiny bit more of a difference.


EDIT: Actually, on my old laptop that has (without turbo-boosting) more stable benchmarks, it's better in all cases.  (of course architectures could behave slightly combines two related things:
1. Make `NpyIter_GetTransferFlags` a public function.  Now that we have these flags in the public API, there seems no reason not to make this public.
2. The iterator currently found the `NEEDS_API` flag every time (with `REFS_OK`) for use in `NpyIter_IterationNeedsAPI`.  But we should generally _not_ use `NpyIter_IterationNeedsAPI` anymore in NumPy.  The reason is that we need to know what buffering needs, but already know anyway if we need the API for whatever we do with the dtypes.  So:
   * Change code to not use it or add a comment for why it is used.
   * Remove the flag and move the logic to `NpyIter_IterationNeedsAPI` so that we avoid the unnecessary flag discovery (at least in throttled CPU mode ~5% of a trivial reduce).

I completely removed one macro, because I didn't want the confusion of which version is used (or two macros).

**This is ready for review, but marking as draft, because the reduce code move is probably only safe after gh-27883.  (if we are lucky, the sanitizer CI run notices uses","user":{"login":"seberg","id":61977,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYxOTc3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/61977?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg","html_url":"https://github.com/seberg","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"This combines two related things:
1. Make `NpyIter_GetTransferFlags` a public function.  Now that we have these flags in the public API, there seems no reason not to make this public.
2. The iterator currently found the `NEEDS_API` flag every time (with `REFS_OK`) for use in `NpyIter_IterationNeedsAPI`.  But we should generally _not_ use `NpyIter_IterationNeedsAPI` anymore in NumPy.  The reason is that we need to know what buffering needs, but already know anyway if we need the API for whatever we do with the dtypes.  So:
   * Change code to not use it or add a comment for why it is used.
   * Remove the flag and move the logic to `NpyIter_IterationNeedsAPI` so that we avoid the unnecessary flag discovery (at least in throttled CPU mode ~5% of a trivial reduce).

I completely removed one macro, because I didn't want the confusion of which version is used (or two macros).

**This is ready for review, but marking as draft, because the reduce code move is probably only safe after gh-27883.  (if we are lucky, the sanitizer CI run notices ... has:
numpy.dtype.type
attribute
dtype.type = None

Is there something missing here?  For example, if I go:
```
>>> import numpy as np
>>> A = np.array([1.0, 2, 3])
>>> A.dtype.type
<class 'numpy.float64'>
>>>
```
...  `<class 'numpy.float64'>` is not `None`?

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/28018","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/28018/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/28018/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/28018/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/28018","id":2744642589,"node_id":"I_kwDOAA3dP86jl-wd","number":28018,"title":"MAINT,ENH: Possible improvements related to buffered iteration","user":{"login":"seberg","id":61977,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYxOTc3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/61977?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg","html_url":"https://github.com/seberg","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/seberg/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-12-17T11:12:01Z","updated_at":"2024-12-17T14:59:21Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"gh-27883 moved setup of buffering to the beginning of the iterator construction to avoid duplicate work later on (and just simplify things a lot).

There are a few related improvements that can be done now:
* The `FixedStridesArray` is now always identical to the actual "inner strides".  There are two things we can do here:
  1. Internally, just stop using the fixed strides (and use the strides immediately) as a slight simplification/optimization.
  2. We could advertise this fact promising to not change it back (I don't _think_ there is much of a reason to consider changing back, but I am not 100% sure.).
* We now know clearly at setup time if we need any buffering at all.
  * We could skip all buffer setup (overhead cost) and use a faster `iternext()` function in principle.  There are two things to keep in mind:
    1. The `BUFFERED` flag is used to reject some API calls, we must keep rejecting the API even if we don't need to buffer internally.
    2. The iterator struct is different when buffering, simply unsetting the flag would break offsets.  (i.e. may need a new flag to just skip steps). 
* The buffered `iternext()` always uses `goto_iterindex`.  This function is heavy weight.  During normal iteration advancing the iterator is much easier and faster (mainly, no need to look at all dimensions).
* The buffer setup is currently unable to realize that it may be beneficial to use a "reduce style" iteration (a double loop) even when not required because doing so may mean fewer operands need to be buffered. [discussion](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27883#discussion_r1865800046)
* The code tries to guess the best buffer-size, but this method is crude and can probably be improved.  A small constraint is that we should err a bit on larger buffers (or we have to deal with floating point precision changes with float16 in the einsum tests).
  The code optimizes "overheads" (very crudely), for small buffers that dominates, but for largish buffers, the buffer copy itself might also make a difference.
  I am sure this can be better, but it is OK if it isn't ideal.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/28018/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/28018/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2548679732","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/28018#issuecomment-2548679732","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/28018","id":2548679732,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP86X6cQ0","user":{"login":"mattip","id":823911,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgyMzkxMQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/823911?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip","html_url":"https://github.com/mattip","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-17T14:59:18Z","updated_at":"2024-12-17T14:59:18Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"Another thing to think about: the buffer size should be adapted to modern CPU cache sizes. ... has:

attribute

```
>>> import numpy as np

>>>

array API standard","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":"PRs and issues related to support for the array API standard"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":11,"created_at":"2019-10-16T19:54:02Z","updated_at":"2024-12-18T18:27:06Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"<!-- Please describe the issue in detail here, and fill in the fields below -->

Currently, in order to sort an array in reverse order you have to use some form of [strange hack](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/26984414/efficiently-sorting-a-numpy-array-in-descending-order), which may or may not be guaranteed to work in the future.

I suggest adding a reverse parameter to np.sort, similar to the native Python sort() function. This would make the hacks unnecessary and improve readability of code, by making the sorting order explicit.

This feature should not result in any performance problems - after all, the sorting function simply have to reverse the boolean result when comparing values.

### Reproducing code example:

<!-- A short code example that reproduces the problem/missing feature. It should be
self-contained, i.e., possible to run as-is via 'python myproblem.py' -->

```python
import numpy as np

a = np.arange(10)
np.random.shuffle(a)
a.sort(reverse = True)
print(a) # should print "[9, 8, 7, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1, 0]"

Describe the issue:

The following code seems correct, and passes type checking under 2.1.3 but not 2.2.0:

```python
import numpy as np

rng = np.random.default_rng(27446968)
n_states = 5

P = rng.random(size=(n_states, n_states))
p = rng.random(n_states)

p = P.T @ p
```

I suppose mypy is flagging that the float width can increase, but since float64 is used by random, that won't happen in this scenario.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
N/A
```


### Error message:

```shell
foo.py:10: error: Incompatible types in assignment (expression has type "ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[floating[Any]]]", variable has type "ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[float64]]")  [assignment]
Found 1 error in 1 file (checked 1 source file)
```


### Python and NumPy Versions:

Python 3.13, mypy 1.13, numpy 2.2.0

files","user":{"login":"mtsokol","id":8431159,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjg0MzExNTk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8431159?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol","html_url":"https://github.com/mtsokol","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtsokol/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"Hi!

This PR is a backward compatible refactor of `np.lib.format` and `np.ctypeslib` to avoid exporting irrelevant objects.

These modules currently export some redundant items, such as:
- `np.lib.format.io`, `np.lib.format.os`, `np.lib.format.warnings`, etc.
- `np.ctypeslib.np` or `np.ctypeslib.os`.

These are mostly details but I think these were missing from Python API cleanup NEP. I moved both entities to dedicated directory/private files. 

AFAIC these changes don't touch the current state of the public API.
